ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tissue prata or roti prata with strawberry topping. FOODON_03302051 (flatbread food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Flat bread) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for tissue prata or roti prata variants in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for tissue prata with strawberry. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving of tissue prata with strawberry is typically one whole piece, weighing approximately 120 g, comprising the thin crispy prata cone or sheet drizzled with strawberry sauce and often condensed milk.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (Plain) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for plain roti prata. Tissue prata is a thinner, crispier variant made from the same dough base but stretched more finely and fried with more ghee, resulting in higher fat and energy per 100 g. Strawberry sauce and condensed milk topping add sugar and carbohydrate. Values are adapted upward for fat and sugar relative to plain prata, consistent with culinary preparation norms. Estimated per-100g values reflect the composite dish including topping. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for flour and ghee component verification.

gi_source.title: Roti / Indian flatbread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tissue prata with strawberry. GI of 70 is estimated based on the GI of plain roti / Indian flatbread (white flour base, GI ~70, high category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The strawberry sauce and condensed milk topping add simple sugars which do not lower the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 55.2) / 100 ≈ 38.6, rounded to 39.5 accounting for topping contribution. Serving carbohydrate = 46.0 g/100g × 120 g serving = 55.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and refined carbohydrate load
hypertension.guidance: Tissue prata with strawberry contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g and is made with ghee and refined white flour; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid additional salty accompaniments such as curry or dhal to keep total sodium intake within recommended daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~70) and high sugar content from strawberry sauce and condensed milk topping
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tissue prata with strawberry has a high GI (~70) and a high glycemic load per serving (~39.5), driven by refined white flour dough and the added strawberry sauce and condensed milk; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish, pairing it with a protein or fibre source if consumed, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and added sugar from topping
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tissue prata with strawberry provides approximately 6.5 g saturated fat and 14 g sugar per 100 g, primarily from ghee used in frying and the strawberry-condensed milk topping; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and prefer plain or egg prata variants with reduced ghee to lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tissue Prata with Strawberry is a popular sweet variant of the classic South Indian-origin flatbread known as roti prata or paratha, widely sold at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and mamak restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. The dough is made from plain white flour, water, ghee, egg, salt, and a small amount of sugar, then rested, stretched extremely thin — much thinner than plain prata — and pan-fried on a flat griddle with generous ghee until it becomes golden, lacy, and crispy throughout. The finished prata is typically shaped into a tall cone or folded into a layered sheet, then drizzled with sweet strawberry sauce and often condensed milk, giving it a dessert-like character. The texture is shatteringly crispy on the outside with a delicate, paper-thin crunch, contrasting with the sweet, fruity, and slightly tangy strawberry topping. Nutritionally, tissue prata with strawberry is energy-dense at approximately 320 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (6.5 g), significant fat (12 g, largely saturated from ghee), and high carbohydrate (46 g) with notable added sugar (14 g) from the topping. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), making it a high-glycemic-load food per serving. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing cardiovascular risk without strict portion control. It is vegetarian and typically halal-certified at hawker stalls. Common meal contexts include breakfast, supper, and as a dessert snack.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for tissue prata with strawberry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any other approved primary source. Values are adapted from HPB plain roti prata data with upward adjustment for additional ghee (tissue preparation method) and added sugar from strawberry sauce and condensed milk topping. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain Indian flatbread/roti, GI ~70) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo plain roti prata entry, adjusted for the tissue prata preparation method (more ghee, thinner dough, crispier texture) and the strawberry sauce plus condensed milk topping. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata and cannot be reliably estimated for this composite dish. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for roti prata. 9. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on plain Indian flatbread (white flour roti) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. 10. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 55.2 g carbohydrate in 120 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.6, reported as 39.5 to account for topping sugar contribution. 11. The input description contained a typo ('strawbery'); corrected to 'strawberry' throughout. 12. cuisine_tags from input included 'Noodle' and 'Fried Rice' which are not relevant to this dish and have been excluded from the output record.
